# Bivariate $q$-normal distribution for transition strengths distribution from many-particle random matrix ensembles generated by $k$-body interactions

> Recently it is established, via lower order moments, that the univariate q-normal distribution, which is the weight function for $q$-Hermite polynomials, describes the ensemble averaged eigenvalue density from many-particle random matrix ensembles generated by $k$-body interactions [Manan Vyas and V.K.B. Kota, J. Stat. Mech. {\bf 2019}, 103103 (2019)]. These ensembles are generically called embedded ensembles of $k$-body interactions [EE($k$)] and their GOE and GUE versions are called EGOE($k$) and EGUE($k$) respectively. Going beyond this work, the lower order bivariate reduced moments of the transition strength densities, generated by EGOE($k$) [or EGUE($k$)] for the Hamiltonian and an independent EGOE($t$) for the transition operator ${\cal O}$ that is $t$-body, are used to establish that the ensemble averaged bivariate transition densities follow the bivariate $q$-normal distribution. Presented are also formulas for the bivariate correlation coefficient $ρ$ and the $q$ values as a function of the particle number $m$, number of single particle states $N$ that the particles are occupying and the body ranks $k$ and $t$ of $H$ and ${\cal O}$ respectively. Finally, using the bivariate $q$ normal form a formula for the chaos measure number of principal components (NPC) in the transition strengths from a state with energy $E$ is presented.

# Bivariate $q$-normal distribution for transition strengths distribution from many-particle random matrix ensembles generated by $k$-body interactions

## Key claim

The ensemble-averaged bivariate transition strength densities generated by embedded Gaussian orthogonal/unitary ensembles [EGOE($k$) or EGUE($k$)] for the Hamiltonian and an independent EGOE($t$) for the transition operator follow a bivariate $q$-normal distribution, with the correspondence holding to within corrections of order 2-5% in the lower-order bivariate moments for typical parameter values.

## Method

The authors computed the lower-order bivariate reduced moments ($\mu_{rs}$ for $r+s \leq 6$) of the bivariate $q$-normal distribution using expansions of powers in terms of $q$-Hermite polynomials and the orthogonality properties of those polynomials. They then compared these analytic moment formulas to the corresponding ensemble-averaged moments derived for EGOE($k$)/EGUE($k$) Hamiltonians with independent EGOE($t$) transition operators, using the binary correlation approximation in the dilute limit with finite-$N$ corrections. The comparison was performed numerically for systems of $m=10$ and $m=15$ fermions with various $k$ and $t$ values.

## Key numbers

- For $m=10$ fermions, $k=2$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.8$, $q = 0.622$; the difference between EGOE and bivariate $q$-normal $\mu_{22}$ is $-0.025$ (about 1.2% relative error)
- For $m=10$ fermions, $k=6$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.4$, $q = 0.0$; the differences between EGOE and bivariate $q$-normal moments are $0.0$ for all computed moments
- For $m=15$ fermions, $k=2$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.867$, $q = 0.743$; the difference in $\mu_{60}$ is $-0.021$ (about 0.2% relative error)
- For $m=15$ fermions, $k=8$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.467$, $q = 0.0$; all moment differences are $0.0$
- For $m=10$ fermions in $N=20$ single-particle states with $k=2$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.682$, $q = 0.465$ (with finite-$N$ corrections)
- For $m=10$ fermions in $N=20$ single-particle states with $k=10$, $t=1$: $\rho = 0.046$, $q = 0.000$
- The $H$ matrix dimension for $m=10$, $N=20$ is $d = \binom{20}{10} = 184756$

## Context

This work extends the earlier result by Vyas and Kota (2019) that the univariate $q$-normal distribution describes the ensemble-averaged eigenvalue density from EGOE($k$) and EGUE($k$). It also generalizes the bivariate Gaussian form for transition strength densities established by French, Kota, and collaborators (FKPT) and Kota and Manan (2015) for the dilute limit with $(k,t) \ll m$, to all $k \leq m$ and $t \leq m$. The bivariate $q$-normal form interpolates between bivariate Gaussian ($q \rightarrow 1$) and bivariate semi-circle ($q=0$) distributions. The results enable calculation of the chaos measure number of principal components (NPC) in transition strengths via an integral formula, with potential applications to nuclear $\beta$-decay rates and neutrinoless double beta decay matrix elements as previously studied by Kota and collaborators.
